analysis:
This question is a red flag disguised as a technical decision. You're asking me to evaluate rewriting a service in Rust, but you've given me zero context about what service we're talking about, what language it's currently written in, what problems it has, who maintains it, how critical it is, or what resources are available. This is exactly how organizations make catastrophically expensive decisions.

Without knowing the current service's performance characteristics, bug history, team expertise, deployment complexity, dependencies, or business criticality, any recommendation is pure speculation. Are we talking about a weekend script or the payment processing backbone? A team of Rust experts or junior developers who barely know the current stack?

The biggest risk here isn't technical - it's organizational. Rewrites are where projects go to die. They consistently take 2-3x longer than estimated, introduce new categories of bugs, and often end up recreating the same architectural problems in a different language. Meanwhile, the old system accumulates technical debt because 'we're rewriting it anyway.'

Rust specifically carries additional risks: steep learning curve, longer development cycles, smaller talent pool for hiring, and ecosystem churn. The benefits - memory safety, performance - only matter if those are actual problems you're solving, not theoretical ones.

findings.title: Insufficient context for decision
findings.detail: No information provided about current service, problems being solved, team capabilities, or business constraints. Making architectural decisions without this context is organizationally dangerous.

findings.title: Rewrite risk not acknowledged
findings.detail: Full rewrites have an extremely high failure rate in the industry. They typically take 2-3x longer than estimated and often recreate existing problems while introducing new ones.

findings.title: Rust-specific adoption risks
findings.detail: Rust has a steep learning curve, smaller talent pool, and longer development cycles. Teams often underestimate the productivity hit during transition period.

findings.title: Opportunity cost blindness
findings.detail: Resources spent on rewriting are resources not spent on features, bug fixes, or other improvements. The question doesn't address what we're not doing instead.

findings.title: Benefits may be theoretical
findings.detail: Rust's advantages (memory safety, performance) only provide value if the current system actually suffers from memory safety issues or performance problems.
